confidence.disclosure: Itinerary reconstructed principally from Ibn Abd al-Hakam's Futuh Misr (the earliest surviving Arabic history of the conquest of Egypt), al-Tabari, and the Coptic chronicle of John of Nikiu. The Egypt conquest sequence is securely dated from 639 to 642. The Libya expeditions and later governorships give a broad framework; some dates for secondary stops are reconstructed from general chronology rather than explicit source citations.

sources: Ibn Abd al-Hakam, Futuh Misr wa Akhbariha (c. 870)
Alfred Butler, The Arab Conquest of Egypt (1902)

journey.event: Born in Mecca into the Banu Sahm clan of Quraysh.
Travelled on a Meccan diplomatic mission to the Negus of Axum in a failed attempt to extradite the Muslim refugees there.
Converted to Islam and joined the Prophet Muhammad in Medina shortly before the conquest of Mecca.
Led a Muslim expedition against hostile tribes on the northern Arabian frontier.
Led a Muslim force into southern Palestine at the start of the Syrian campaigns.
Defeated a Byzantine force near Gaza (Dathin) in early 634.
Took part in the Battle of Ajnadayn, a major Muslim victory in Palestine.
Commanded a division at the Battle of Yarmuk that broke Byzantine power in Syria.
Crossed into Egypt with a small force of ~4,000 men in December 639, beginning the Arab conquest.
Captured the Byzantine frontier fortress of Pelusium after a month-long siege.
Took Bilbeis in the eastern Nile delta after a short siege.
Defeated the Byzantine field army at the Battle of Heliopolis in July 640.
Besieged and took the Roman fortress of Babylon on the Nile in April 641, the future site of Fustat.
Defeated Byzantine forces at the Battle of Nikiou on the Nile in May 641.
Accepted the surrender of Alexandria in September 642 after negotiation with the Byzantine patriarch Cyrus.
Founded Fustat as the first Muslim garrison city and capital of Egypt on the site of his siege camp.
Led the first Muslim expedition into Cyrenaica and made a treaty with the local population.
Besieged and took Tripoli in western Libya.
Retook Alexandria after a Byzantine naval expedition under Manuel briefly recaptured the city.
Served as chief negotiator for Mu'awiya at the arbitration following the Battle of Siffin.
Took part in the Adhruh arbitration between supporters of Ali and Mu'awiya.
Returned to Egypt as governor for Mu'awiya after defeating Ali's appointee Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr.
Died in Fustat in 664 while serving as governor of Egypt under Mu'awiya.
